Active Work Architecture
─[ Messaging Kit ]─ ┐

Messaging Kit

Positioning reference for all launch assets. Every claim is grounded in shipped code — no aspirational features. For code-level evidence backing each claim, see technical-claims.md.

The Problem

AI coding agents start every session from zero. They don’t know your project, your conventions, your infrastructure, or the snags, gotchas, quirks you hit yesterday. You re-explain the same things. You watch the agent make the same mistakes. Context that should compound gets thrown away.

Developers work around this with static instruction files (CLAUDE.md, .cursorrules) they manually maintain. But those files don’t grow with usage, don’t enforce anything, don’t share across platforms, and don’t capture what the agent learns during work.

Positioning

Persistent memory for AI coding agents.

Uses “memory” because it’s the concept users immediately understand. Technically it’s context injection, not recall — address this honestly in docs and deeper content, but don’t lead with the caveat. “Knowledge” is more precise but less intuitive as a one-liner.

Extended (two sentences):

Persistent memory for AI coding agents. Lore captures skills, conventions, and project knowledge as you work — then loads them every session so your agent gets smarter instead of starting over.

Key Messages

Five benefit statements a user could repeat to a colleague. Each maps to real behavior.

1. “Sessions accelerate instead of resetting”

Every session opens with your full project context: identity, conventions, active work, available agents, knowledge map, and local memory. The agent already knows what your project is, how to behave, and what’s in progress. No re-explaining.

Evidence: Session-init hook builds and injects a context banner from ~10 project files at every startup, resume, and context compaction.

2. “Snags become fieldnotes that persist across sessions”

When your agent hits an API quirk, an encoding edge case, or a deployment snag, it captures that as a fieldnote — a structured markdown file that loads in every future session. The mistake happens once, the fix persists.

Evidence: Fieldnotes live in .lore/fieldnotes/ with structured frontmatter. They sync to all platform-specific locations and appear in the session banner’s knowledge map.

3. “Conventions are enforced, not just documented”

Your coding standards, docs rules, and security policies aren’t just written down — they’re injected before every file write. The agent sees the relevant conventions right when it matters.

Evidence: Convention guard hook extracts principle lines from convention files and injects them as PreToolUse context. Different conventions surface for different file paths (docs rules for docs/, security rules for all writes).

4. “One knowledge base, every platform, every repo”

Capture a skill in Claude Code — it’s available in Cursor and OpenCode. Link five repos to one hub — they all share the same knowledge. No copying, no drift.

Evidence: Platform sync scripts translate canonical .lore/ content to platform-specific formats. Hub linking resolves all knowledge from a central location.

5. “Complex work delegates to focused workers”

When work benefits from a fresh context window, the orchestrator spawns worker agents loaded with curated skills and conventions. Compound tasks split across parallel workers for maximum throughput. Each worker gets exactly the context it needs — no more, no less.

Evidence: The orchestrator selects skills from skills-registry.md and spawns lore-worker-agent with task-specific context. The session banner surfaces the delegation model. Workers carry model preferences per platform. Operator agents are optional for recurring patterns.

Personas

Solo developer with AI coding agents

Pain: “I spend the first 10 minutes of every session re-explaining my project.” “My agent keeps making the same mistakes.” “I have .cursorrules files scattered across 8 repos.”

Message: Lore makes your agent remember. Skills, conventions, and project context persist across sessions. One hub shares knowledge to all your repos.

Hook: “Your agent gets smarter every session instead of starting from zero.”

Developer on a team using AI agents

Pain: “Every developer’s agent behaves differently.” “There’s no consistency in how agents write code or docs.” “I can’t see what context my agent is getting.”

Message: Each developer runs their own Lore instance with shared conventions and skills checked into git. Same knowledge, same rules, every session. Full audit trail via git history.

Hook: “Consistent AI behavior across your team — shared through git, enforced by hooks.”

Infrastructure / DevOps engineer

Pain: “My environment has 40 services and the agent forgets all of them between sessions.” “I re-explain the same network topology every time.” “Runbooks exist but the agent doesn’t know about them.”

Message: Lore maps your environment as you work. Services, endpoints, procedures, and snags accumulate in structured docs that load every session. Your agent knows your infrastructure.

Hook: “Your agent knows your infrastructure without being told twice.”

Elevator Pitches

10 seconds:

“Lore gives AI coding agents persistent memory — skills, fieldnotes, and project knowledge survive across sessions so your agent gets smarter over time.”

30 seconds:

“Every AI coding session starts from zero — your agent doesn’t know your project, your conventions, or the snags you hit yesterday. Lore fixes that. It captures skills, fieldnotes, and knowledge as you work, then loads them every session. Conventions are enforced at write-time, and one knowledge base syncs across Claude Code, Cursor, and OpenCode. Plain files, git-versioned, zero dependencies.”

2 minutes:

“AI coding agents have a memory problem. Every session starts blank. You re-explain your project, your agent makes the same mistakes, and context that should compound gets thrown away. Developers work around this with instruction files they maintain by hand, but those don’t grow, don’t enforce anything, and don’t share.

Lore is a persistent knowledge layer for AI coding agents. When your agent hits a snag, it captures that as a fieldnote — a structured markdown file that loads in every future session. Conventions aren’t just documented, they’re injected before every file write. Your project identity, active work, environment docs, and agent roster all surface at session start.

As your knowledge grows, the orchestrator delegates to focused workers loaded with curated skills and fieldnotes. It works across platforms — same knowledge base in Claude Code, Cursor, and OpenCode. Link multiple repos to one hub and they all share the same skills, fieldnotes, and context.

It’s plain files in a git repo. Zero runtime dependencies. Setup is npx create-lore and you’re running. Everything is auditable — you can read every hook, diff every change, and see exactly what your agent sees.”

Objection Handling

“Why not just use CLAUDE.md / .cursorrules?”

Those are static files you maintain by hand. Lore starts there but adds three things: fieldnotes that accumulate from real work (not just instructions you write upfront), conventions enforced at write-time (not just documented), and cross-platform sync (write once, available everywhere). If your static file works fine, you don’t need Lore yet. When you find yourself maintaining the same snags across multiple projects, that’s when it clicks.

“Isn’t Lore more expensive because it keeps injecting context instead of loading once?”

Sometimes yes, if implemented badly. But “loaded once” is often misunderstood: most agent stacks still resend system/instruction context on later turns, and large static files can create a persistent token tax too. Lore’s target pattern is full orientation once, then compact reminders on later turns. Example: OpenCode session transform now does full banner on first call and compact one-liner after. Cost should be judged from measurements (LORE_HOOK_LOG=1 + scripts/analyze-hook-logs.sh), not assumptions about one-time injection.

“Do large AGENTS.md setups still compete with Lore for complex work?”

Yes. Well-maintained AGENTS.md setups can work very well. Lore’s advantage is not that static files fail; it’s that Lore keeps context structured and operational under session pressure: compaction re-orientation, adaptive capture nudges, write-time convention guidance, and cross-platform consistency. If you don’t need those, a static setup may remain the better simplicity tradeoff.

“What if Claude/Cursor add native memory?”

Platform memory solves single-session recall — “remember I prefer TypeScript.” Lore solves structured knowledge that compounds: fieldnotes from real snags, environment maps, runbooks, convention enforcement, domain delegation. These are complementary. If platforms add memory that makes some of Lore’s features redundant, that’s a win — less to maintain.

“This is over-engineered.”

Setup is one command. The directory structure is markdown files. Hooks are plain JavaScript you can read in 5 minutes. Complexity grows only as your knowledge does. If you delete Lore, you delete files — no accounts, no services, no cleanup.

“One developer, no adoption proof.”

Honest: it’s early. The framework works — CI is green across 3 OS, 2 Node versions, 3 platforms. Adoption is unproven. Try it, read the hooks, decide for yourself.

“I don’t want hooks injecting stuff into my agent.”

Every hook is readable JavaScript. The session banner is plaintext you can inspect. The only hard block is preventing writes to MEMORY.md (routing to structured locations instead). Everything else is context injection — the agent sees more information, not less. You can disable any hook by removing it from the config.

Honest Limitations

These appear in launch content. Hiding them erodes trust faster than any feature builds it.

  1. Knowledge capture is nudged, not forced. Hooks remind the agent to document what it learns. The agent can ignore them. The only hard enforcement is MEMORY.md routing.
  2. Single-developer tool. No multi-user locking or access control. Designed for individual use. Team sharing works through git — each developer runs their own instance with shared conventions committed to the repo.
  3. 0.x maturity. Knowledge format and file structure are stable. Hook APIs and scripts may change before 1.0. Operator-owned files are never touched by framework updates.
  4. Context window cost. The session banner consumes tokens. Large knowledge bases mean larger banners. Lore should use full-at-start plus compact-per-turn patterns where possible, but operators still own their baseline measurement (LORE_HOOK_LOG=1 + scripts/analyze-hook-logs.sh).
  5. Platform APIs are unstable. Cursor’s hook system has known gaps (a workaround MCP server exists). Platform changes can break adapters.
  6. No semantic search. Knowledge is injected as full text at session start, not retrieved on demand. Simple but doesn’t scale to very large knowledge bases.
  7. Requires Node.js 18+ and git. No browser-based or cloud setup. Users need a local development environment.

Tone

Sound like: A senior engineer explaining their side project to another senior engineer. Direct, specific, honest about tradeoffs.

Do:

  • Lead with the problem, then the outcome. “Sessions start from zero” → “sessions accelerate.”
  • State limitations before the user finds them. Trust compounds faster than hype.
  • Use specifics. “3 platforms, zero dependencies, 60-second setup” — not “comprehensive and easy.”
  • Let users verify. “Read the hooks — they’re 50-line JavaScript files.”
  • Use “memory” in headlines and positioning (it’s what users understand), but explain the mechanism honestly in docs and deeper content.

Don’t:

  • Promise time savings without measured evidence.
  • Use “revolutionary”, “game-changing”, “the future of”, or any superlative.
  • Compare to enterprise knowledge management. Different problem, different scale.
  • Claim the agent “learns” — the system captures and reinjects, the agent doesn’t retain anything on its own.

Competitive Framing

AlternativeWhat it doesWhere Lore differs
CLAUDE.md / .cursorrulesStatic instruction file, manually maintainedDynamic context that grows with usage, conventions enforced at write-time, cross-platform sync, and explicit compaction recovery
Platform native memorySingle-platform, flat text, no structureStructured by type (skills/fieldnotes/rules/environment), git-versioned, multi-platform, shareable
Custom system promptsCopy-paste per sessionAutomatic injection, project-aware, compounds over time
RAG toolsSemantic search over documentsComplementary — Lore captures and structures, works without infrastructure
Shared team wikisManual documentation siloAgent-accessible, injected automatically, conventions enforced not just documented

Channel Lines

README: “Persistent memory for AI coding agents. Plain files, git-versioned, zero dependencies.”

HN: “Show HN: Lore — Persistent memory for AI coding agents”

r/ClaudeAI: “I built a knowledge framework that makes Claude Code remember everything across sessions — snags become fieldnotes, rules are enforced, complex work delegates to focused workers”

r/LocalLLaMA: “Persistent memory for AI coding agents — git-native, no SaaS, zero deps. Works with Claude Code, Cursor, and OpenCode.”

Blog: “Every AI coding session starts from zero. Here’s what I built to fix that — and what it can’t do.”

PgUp/PgDn: scroll b: bulk commands q: quit x: menu ← → ↑ ↓: navigate
Donate 0.24.4
